Savant

Branding and product design for a neurodivergent ecosystem.

Timeline 3 months (phase one of ongoing work)
Role Sole Designer · Brand & Product (discovery, identity, UX/UI)
Client Savant, a neurodivergent ecosystem (real client, ongoing)
Scope Brand discovery · logo & submark · brand system · Ambassador module UX
Tools Figma · Excel
Overview

From a mission without a face to a brand and a product flow that could carry it.

Savant is a digital ecosystem built to empower neurodivergent individuals and their families across assessment, professionals, community, and employment. The mission was clear; the visual language wasn't keeping up with it, and a key onboarding module wasn't pulling its weight.

I worked across two phases, solo. First, a brand refresh: running a stakeholder discovery workshop, then redesigning the identity from a generic mark into something that actually means Savant. Second, the UX redesign of the "Ambassador" module, the flow responsible for bringing patrons, mentors, and parent advisors onto the platform.

The thread across both: taking something well-intentioned but underbuilt and giving it clarity, visually and functionally. This was the first shipped phase of what's become ongoing work with the client.

The Starting Point

A clear mission, carried by an identity that didn't fit it.

Savant arrived with real purpose but no real brand identity to carry it. What existed leaned on bright blue and pink, default-feeling colors chosen without a clear rationale, signaling nothing about who the platform was actually for. There was no system underneath, and the logo carried no connection to what "Savant" means or who it serves. It was a placeholder, not a brand.

The product had a parallel gap. The Ambassador module, the flow meant to bring patrons, professional mentors, and parent advisors onto the platform, was one of Savant's most important growth channels, but it didn't work like one. There was no clear introduction to what it was, no real onboarding, and all three roles were funneled through a single generic questionnaire. A critical feature was being treated like an afterthought.

Two problems, one root: a platform whose impact had outgrown the design meant to carry it.

The starting point: the old logo, landing page, and Ambassador module. A brand and product that didn't yet match the mission.

Brand Discovery

Four stakeholders, no easy way to get them in one room.

Before designing anything, I needed alignment, and that was the first real challenge. Four key stakeholders, each with their own view of what Savant should be, and no feasible way to run a live session together.

So I designed around the constraint. Instead of a workshop nobody could attend, I built a structured discovery questionnaire, guiding each stakeholder through Savant's vision, values, and audience independently. It gave everyone equal input, avoided the loudest-voice-wins dynamic of a group call, and let me synthesize patterns into an evidence-based direction rather than a compromise.

The questionnaire surfaced something more useful than a single answer: it surfaced a tension. On visual style, most reached for "colorful and playful," the familiar look of the space. But on how they wanted users to feel, the answers turned quieter: clarity, relief, trust, calm, belonging. They were caught between wanting to feel approachable and needing to feel trustworthy; energetic, but not so playful it stopped being taken seriously.

A competitor scan sharpened the opportunity: nearly everyone in the neurodiversity space looked the same, vibrant, playful, white backgrounds. That gap pointed somewhere clear. Savant could be the one that felt warm and human and grounded enough to trust, resolving the stakeholders' own tension instead of picking a side.

The workshop, distilled: calm but colorful, simple but distinctive, and a unanimous vote for friendly over corporate.

The competitive landscape: white backgrounds and bright accents everywhere, which shaped where Savant could stand apart.

The Identity

A mark that means Savant, not just one that spells it.

The old logo was a placeholder; the new one needed to carry meaning. So I started with the word itself. A savant is someone with deep, often extraordinary ability, and "savant syndrome" describes remarkable skills, frequently in pattern and detail, that often coexist with neurodivergence. That was the idea worth building on: not deficit, but a different and powerful kind of mind.

The puzzle is a familiar shape in the neurodiversity space, but I didn't want to use it unconsidered. My first direction tried to honor two things at once: the puzzle the stakeholders connected with, and the celebratory, diverse spirit of the rainbow infinity symbol the community uses to represent itself. The result was a colorful puzzle, vibrant and playful, matching what stakeholders had asked for in discovery, while nodding to how the community represents its own diversity.

The client pushed back. They worried the colorful mark read as too playful to feel professional, the same approachable-versus-trustworthy tension discovery had surfaced, now playing out in the logo itself. I saw it differently, but it was their brand to own. So I found a path that respected their call without losing the energy: I refined the puzzle into a cleaner, more minimal mark for the professionalism they wanted, even building a custom typeface to make the wordmark fully Savant's own, and moved the color and playfulness into the wider website, letting the community's vibrancy live across the experience rather than in the logo alone.

Alongside the primary logo, I built a submark for smaller and more versatile applications, so the identity holds up everywhere from a favicon to a banner.

From paper, to grayscale exploration, to the first colorful puzzle direction: the mark finding its shape.

The palette and type carried the resolution. Warmth and color to stay approachable and human across the experience; restraint in the mark itself to feel trustworthy: a brand Savant could own in a space where nearly everyone looks the same.

Warm enough to belong, grounded enough to trust.

The Ambassador Module

One form for three very different people. That was the problem.

With the brand in place, I moved to product. The Ambassador module is how Savant brings its key people onboard: Patrons who fund and champion it, Professional Mentors who lend expertise, and Parent Advisors who guide from lived experience. It was one of the platform's most important growth channels, and it wasn't working like one.

The core issue was that three very different people were being treated as one. A single generic questionnaire asked everyone the same things, with a role dropdown bolted on: no real introduction to what the module was, no sense of why each role mattered, and no guidance tailored to who you actually were. A Patron and a Parent Advisor have almost nothing in common in what they need to know, yet the flow couldn't tell them apart.

I worked through it with a Double Diamond approach: auditing the existing module, mapping each role's distinct needs, then restructuring around them. The redesign gave the module a proper landing experience that explained its purpose, then tailored the onboarding per role, each path opening with an explanation written for that person and asking the questions that actually mattered to them, while keeping the shared basics shared. A Patron and a Parent Advisor now get a flow that speaks to them, not a generic form with a dropdown.

The redesign was presented to stakeholders and approved without major revisions, a sign the structure matched what the platform actually needed. It's currently being built.

Paper first, pixels second: sketches on the left, wireframes and the connected hi-fi prototype on the right.

The details doing the work: the significant design decisions inside the Ambassador module.

Reflection

Brand and product are the same job, seen from two angles.

The most valuable part of Savant was the range it demanded. Moving from a brand workshop to a logo to a product flow meant switching between very different modes of thinking: strategic and emotional one week, structural and functional the next. What held it together was realizing they're the same job: understand who you're serving, then reduce the gap between what they need and what they get. Doing both, solo, taught me more than either would have alone.

The project also sharpened how I work with stakeholders. When the client and I saw the logo's color differently, I didn't dig in: I showed them options and the reasoning behind each, and we landed on a compromise neither of us would have reached alone: a simpler, more grounded mark, with the color and energy moved into the wider site. Disagreement, handled with options instead of opinions, usually makes the work better.

Designing in this space also kept me learning. I've since come to understand how actively many autistic self-advocates reject the puzzle symbol, reading it as implying incompleteness, and how the community-led rainbow infinity has become the preferred alternative. If I were advising Savant today, I'd bring that to the table. It's a reminder that inclusive design isn't only visual; it means staying close to how a community represents itself.

This was the first shipped phase of ongoing work with the client, with the home page and further modules still ahead.
